_**User (2026-07-10 08:16:30Z)**_

where is the virtual device tv?

---

_**Agent (copilot/auto)**_

I'll show where the Vega virtual device is in Vega Studio and how to open or start it.- In VS Code open the Vega Studio sidebar (the Vega/robot icon in the activity bar).
- Expand the DEVICES section — you should see an entry named "VirtualDevice:Tv" (Mode: developer).
- To show the emulator window, click the small play/open icon (">" / launch) to the right of that device entry. The emulator window title is "Vega Virtual Device".
- If you don't see it, check macOS Mission Control or the app windows (it may be behind other apps).
- If the device is not listed or is stopped:
  - In Vega Studio set Build Mode → Debug, then use the Devices panel controls to start or reinstall the virtual device.
  - If SDKs are missing, open the SDK MANAGER area in Vega Studio and install the required image, then start the device.
- Quick checks:
  - The emulator window title: "Vega Virtual Device".
  - The device entry shows its mode (developer vs production) and has start/stop/launch buttons.
  - Use the small device row buttons to open logs, inspector, or to restart the virtual device.

If you want, I can:
- Give exact Vega CLI commands to list/start devices if you prefer CLI control.
- Walk you through launching the device from Vega Studio step-by-step while you follow along. Which would you like?

---

VirtualDevice:Tv (Not running)

---

_**Agent (copilot/auto)**_

Yes — that screenshot shows the virtual device is present but stopped: "VirtualDevice:Tv (Not running)".

What to do next
- In Vega Studio set **Build Modes → Debug** (already visible in your screenshot).
- Start the device: click the device row and press the play/launch icon (or right‑click the entry → Start). The device state should change to running.
- Open the emulator window: click the small open/launch icon to the right of the device row (the emulator window title is "Vega Virtual Device").
- Once it’s running: open the app on the device, open the dev menu (remote Menu button or press `d` in the Metro terminal), set the debug server host to `<your-mac-ip>:8081`, enable Fast Refresh, then Reload.

If the Start/Launch buttons are disabled or no image is available:
- Install the required Vega SDK image via the SDK MANAGER section in Vega Studio, then start the device.
- Or tell me and I’ll prepare the exact Vega CLI commands to start/install the virtual device for you. Which do you want me to do?

---

_**Agent (copilot/auto)**_

I'll give targeted troubleshooting steps to get the device connected to Metro, verify Fast Refresh, and what to paste back so I can diagnose.- Quick goal: make the Vega Virtual Device load JS from Metro so Fast Refresh/HMR works.

Steps to perform now (copy/paste where needed)

1) Confirm app is running on the device
- Use the virtual remote to navigate to your app tile and press OK so the app is foregrounded.

2) Open the in-app dev menu and set Metro host
- On the virtual remote press the Menu (three lines) button.
- Dev menu → Dev Settings → Debug server host & port for device → enter:
  <your-mac-ip>:8081
- Get your Mac IP:
```bash
ipconfig getifaddr en0
# if empty try:
ipconfig getifaddr en1
```

3) Enable Fast Refresh and Reload
- In the dev menu toggle **Fast Refresh** ON.
- Choose **Reload**.

4) Test HMR
- Edit `src/data/tiles.tsx` (change the `label` to some unique text), save.
- Watch the Metro terminal for HMR logs and watch the emulator update without a full rebuild.

What to look for in Metro terminal
- Successful connection: lines like “Client connected”, “Connected to <ip>”, or a Fast Refresh/HMR message.
- HMR success: “Fast Refresh performed” or “HMR: applied patch”.
- Errors: syntax/type errors will appear and block HMR.

If it doesn’t connect
- Confirm app is a Debug build (not a release bundle). In Vega Studio set Build Mode → Debug and reinstall the app, or run:
```bash
npm run build:debug
```
(If you want, I can run that here — say “Build debug” and approve unsandboxed execution.)

- Confirm device can reach your Mac: same network, no firewall blocking port 8081.

If Metro still logs “No apps connected” after Reload
- Paste the last ~30 lines of the Metro terminal here.
- Also paste (or screenshot) the dev-menu screen showing the Debug server host & port you set.

Optional checks in Vega Studio
- Use the Device / App logs or Activity Monitor in Vega Studio to see runtime errors.
- Use Chrome Dev Tools (Vega Studio → Chrome Dev Tools) if you enabled remote JS debugging (note: remote debug changes runtime semantics; prefer Fast Refresh).

If you prefer, I can:
- Run `npm run build:debug` here (need your approval for unsandboxed run), or
- Guide you step‑by‑step while you paste Metro output after you Reload.

Which do you want me to do next, and paste the Metro output after you press Reload?
